candidate_problems.text: What precise empirical or formal criterion would distinguish genuinely emergent semantics from merely sophisticated statistical pattern-tracking, if computational irreducibility blocks closed-form derivations? The conjecture needs a non-question-begging test for when 'stable referential, inferential, and action-guiding structures' count as meaning rather than just useful internal correlations.

What is the minimal generative architecture required for meaning to emerge, and which components are actually necessary: prediction, compression, error-correction, embodiment, intervention, social interaction, or persistent world-modeling? The conjecture asserts such an architecture exists but leaves open the boundary between sufficient and indispensable mechanisms.

How can semantics be said to refer to the world, rather than only to internally useful attractors, if meaning is a dynamically maintained achievement of closed feedback loops? The unresolved tension is whether world-coupled success is enough to ground aboutness, truth, and misrepresentation, or whether the view still smuggles in the semantic notions it aims to explain.
